html:
Cibox Inter@ctive is not the hidden cybersecurity SaaS story in the prior draft. That was wrong. The real thesis is stranger and more interesting: a forgotten French penny stock, listed as GETTEX:1YH / Euronext Growth:ALCBX, is trying to turn itself from a busted consumer electronics distributor into a domestic European e-bike industrial platform.

The market is pricing the company as a serially diluted micro-cap with losses, ORA financing, weak liquidity, and a terrible chart. That part is fair. At roughly €0.01165 per share, using the 817.6 million shares reported at year-end 2025, the equity value is about €9.5 million. But the variant perception is that Cibox is no longer just selling imported scooters and gadgets. It now has a live factory in Revin, France, 92 employees at year-end 2025, roughly 100 plus currently, a stated 50,000 e-bike production objective for 2026, and a path to deeper industrial integration through painting, frames, and battery packs.

2025 looked ugly on reported earnings but materially better on strategic direction. Revenue rose 50% to €14.65 million, gross margin improved to 26.6% from 22.9%, and e-bikes became about 80% of sales while international revenue reached nearly 70%. The loss widened because Cibox staffed and ramped the Revin plant before the production base was mature: EBITDA was -€2.9 million and net income -€3.6 million. That is the optical bear case. The forward case is that 2025 was the expense year and 2026 is the first real utilization year.

The most important datapoint is Q1 2026. Revenue doubled to €3.2 million, Cibox assembled more than 12,500 bikes in the quarter, sold and delivered over 5,600, and had 5,800 ready to deliver into the industrial order book plus 1,500 units available for direct channels. Management says Q2 production should be above Q1 and still confirms more than 50,000 bikes for 2026. If Cibox delivers even 40,000 to 50,000 bikes at roughly €500 to €600 manufacturer revenue per unit, 2026 revenue can land around €25 million to €32 million before giving much credit to scooters, services, repair, reconditioning, or third-party industrial work.

That matters because the fixed-cost base is already largely visible. In 2025, gross profit was €3.9 million against personnel expense of €3.0 million and external charges of €4.1 million. At €30 million of revenue and a 27% to 29% gross margin, gross profit moves toward €8 million to €9 million. That is close to EBITDA breakeven even before the benefits of higher utilization, less start-up inefficiency, and later process integration. At €40 million plus revenue, the operating leverage becomes real.

The misunderstood asset is the factory option. The Revin site is not just a brand story. It gives Cibox a “made in France / made in Europe” answer at a time when retailers, mobility brands, and public-sector buyers want lower Asia dependence, shorter lead times, traceability, and repairability. The local Ardennes government says the site could ultimately scale to 150,000 bikes per year and around 250 employees. The company’s own 2026 plan is more modest at 50,000, but the asymmetry comes from buying the first 50,000-unit ramp at a valuation that assumes the plant remains a cash-burning experiment.

The market backdrop is bad, which is why the opportunity exists. French bicycle sales kept falling in 2025, e-bike volume dropped to about 507,000 units, and inventories across the sector remain heavy. But Cibox is not trying to win by betting on a hot retail cycle. It is trying to become a low-cost, flexible European assembly and service partner while selling its own Yeep.me brand through marketplaces and retail channels. Germany, Belgium, Italy, and the Netherlands already drove the international acceleration in 2025. Germany remains Europe’s key e-bike market, with around 2 million e-bikes sold in 2025, and the Netherlands still has e-bikes at roughly half of unit sales and most of bike revenue. Cibox does not need the French market to boom. It needs enough European retailers and brands to prefer nearby assembly over long import chains.

The balance sheet is the central risk. Cibox had €7.1 million of equity at year-end 2025, €6.3 million of net inventory, €1.0 million of cash and marketable securities, and €2.2 million of financial debt. Since then, it raised €1.9 million gross via LITA.co from more than 1,500 subscribers and renewed a €2 million ORA program, with a €0.5 million first draw. This keeps the ramp funded, but it also keeps dilution risk front and center. The March 2026 ORA table showed 234 ORA still outstanding and 83.4 million shares already created through ORA reimbursement. The bear argument is simple: if working capital keeps consuming cash before the factory hits volume, shareholders get diluted again and again.

Retail sentiment understands this. Boursorama forum posts focus on the annual report, the 50,000-bike ambition, and the fear that Cibox cannot “survive forever” on ORA/OCA financing without bringing in a strategic partner. German retail visibility is tiny, with BoersenNEWS showing only a handful of stale comments. That is a useful signal. This is not a crowded turnaround. It is a neglected, dilution-scarred micro-cap where the next re-rating requires evidence, not narrative.

The best version of the thesis is a strategic-partner thesis, not just a retail-brand thesis. If Revin proves it can produce 50,000 bikes at acceptable quality and gross margin, Cibox becomes more valuable to a larger European bike, mobility, logistics, or retail group than it is as a standalone listed penny stock. A partner could provide working capital, orders, purchasing scale, and credibility. The current market cap is too small relative to the operational footprint if the plant works.

Base case: Cibox produces near 50,000 bikes in 2026, ships enough to reach €28 million to €32 million of revenue, approaches EBITDA breakeven, and shows that inventory is converting into cash rather than becoming obsolete. On 0.8x to 1.0x forward sales, adjusted for remaining dilution and debt, the stock is worth about €0.030. From €0.01165, that is roughly 157% upside.

Bull case: the factory proves strategic, 2027 revenue pushes toward €50 million plus, gross margin moves above 30% as painting, frames, batteries, 3R services, and third-party production contribute, and a strategic investor or acquirer removes the financing overhang. At €5 million to €7 million of forward EBITDA or roughly 1x sales, the stock can trade around €0.080. That is about 587% upside.

Bear case: Q2/Q3 shipments disappoint, the French and European e-bike market stay overstocked, the 50,000-unit production target becomes inventory rather than revenue, and ORA/convertible financing continues at distressed prices. In that case, tangible book value is consumed by losses and dilution, and the stock can drift to €0.004. That is about 66% downside.

Current stock price: about €0.01165. Bear target: €0.004. Base target: €0.030. Bull target: €0.080.
